doctrinal_renderings.advaita: The svabhava-born (own-nature-born) duties of the kshatriya — saura (heroism), tejas (bold ardor that cannot be overborne), dhrti (steadiness sustaining one through every condition), daksya (swift competence in sudden affairs without confusion), non-retreat in battle, dana (open-handedness), and isvarabhava (the making-manifest of lordly power toward those to be governed) — are here enumerated not as ends but as the purifying field of niskama-karma. Sankara reads each term precisely: dhrti is that by which one does not collapse in any state; daksya is un-bewildered action when circumstances press suddenly. These are the karma assigned by svabhava to the kshatriya-birth, and performed without fruit-desire they thin the veil before jnana.
doctrinal_renderings.viśiṣṭādvaita: Ramanuja opens each term toward Bhagavan: saura is the capacity to enter battle without fear, teja is the quality of being un-overpowered by adversaries, and dhrti is the power to complete what has been undertaken even when obstacles interrupt it. Dana is not mere generosity but the transfer of what one regards as one's own into the ownership of another, a rehearsal of the deeper surrender of the self to Isvara. Isvarabhava — the power to govern and regulate all beings other than oneself — is assigned to the kshatriya as a delegated shadow of Bhagavan's own sovereignity. All eight qualities together form the kshatriya's kainkarya (service-mode) by which this varna moves toward bhakti-yoga.
doctrinal_renderings.dvaita: Madhvacarya's commentary on this sloka is absent from the transmitted corpus; this rendering is inferred from his broader framework. The eight kshatriya svabhava-dharmas are expressions of the jiva's dependence on Hari: heroism and lordly power do not belong to the kshatriya as self-sufficient qualities but flow through him as Hari wills, reflecting Hari's own omnipotence (sarvottamatva). Dana performed as offering to Hari, and apalayana (non-flight in battle) performed as Hari's instrument, constitute the dependent jiva's proper mode — never confusing the delegated capacity for self-originating power. [Bhāṣya absent: Madhva corpus records no comment on BG 18.43; inference from established Dvaita praxis only.]
doctrinal_renderings.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's terse commentary names the essentials — dhrti as dairya (fortitude, not mere endurance), daksya as battle-competence, isvarabhava as aisvarya (lordliness) — and reads them as svabhava flowing from Krsna's own lila-prasada into the kshatriya's nature. In Pustimarga the kshatriya's martial gifts are not earned or cultivated but gifted by Krsna's grace as the shape of this particular jiva's participation in divine play. Even dana, the open hand, is Krsna's own generosity moving through a human channel; the kshatriya who recognizes this performs his svadharma as an act of surrender rather than self-assertion.
doctrinal_renderings.bhakti: Sridhara Svami reads all eight qualities as svabhavikas (natural, arising from innate constitution) for the kshatriya: saura is parakrama (valor), teja is pragalbhya (boldness), dhrti is dairya, daksya is kausala (skill), apalayana is paranmukha-ta (not turning the face away from the enemy), dana is audarya (magnanimity), and isvarabhava is niyamana-sakti (the power of governance). He offers a clean philological survey without collapsing toward any single darshana, treating the verse as straightforward varna-dharma instruction. The bhakta performing these duties with devotion to Isvara fulfills both svadharma and the path of surrender.
doctrinal_renderings.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudan Sarasvati supplies the richest analytical glosses: saura is vikrama — the disposition to strike even those stronger than oneself; teja is pragalbhya, the quality of being un-dominated by adversaries (parairadharsaniyatva); dhrti is the non-collapse of the body-sense-aggregate even in great calamity; daksya is prompt, unconfused action when urgent affairs arise. He adds that kshatriya-karma is sattvopasarjana-rajoguna-svabhavajam — born of rajoguna with sattva as support — locating it within guna-theory. For Madhusudan, performing these with Krsna as the inner sovereign (antaryamin) transforms martial svadharma into both jnana-purification and bhakti-offering, honoring both his Advaita inheritance and his Vaisnava devotion.

so_what_questions: Krsna lists eight distinct qualities rather than one — what does the granularity itself reveal about how the Gita understands svadharma, and why cannot a single term like 'duty' capture it?

Isvarabhava (the quality of being a lord, a governor) appears alongside dana (generosity) — what is the relationship between power and gift-giving in kshatriya ethics, and where does that pairing break down in modern institutional life?
If dhrti means sustaining the body-sense aggregate without collapse in great calamity, how is that different from repression or mere stoicism, and what enables the distinction?
Daksya is defined as acting without confusion (avyamoha) when sudden urgent circumstances press — where in your own work do you most often lose that quality, and what is the obstacle?

Apalayana (non-flight from battle) is listed as a natural quality, not a command — what is the difference between svabhava-driven steadiness and rule-driven obligation, and which is more reliable under pressure?

Vallabha reads all eight as Krsna's prasada flowing through the kshatriya; Sankara reads them as karma purifying toward jnana. Both use the same verse, same words. What determines which reading a reader reaches, and is that determination itself a form of svabhava?

everyday_applications.advaita: Perform the full scope of your professional role — leadership, difficult decisions, generosity with credit, not retreating from conflict — as niskama-karma: the fruit is not yours to hold. The point is the purification of the instrument, not the outcome.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: When you exercise authority or governance over others — a team, a household, an institution — recognize isvarabhava as a delegated shadow of Bhagavan's sovereignty. You are not the lord; you are the lord's steward. That recognition reshapes how power is held.
everyday_applications.dvaita: In any moment of genuine heroism or generosity, pause and locate the source: this capacity is not self-generated. Practice returning each act of valor or dana mentally to Hari as its actual author, collapsing the fiction of self-sufficiency.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Let your natural gifts — whatever your svabhava actually is — flow without anxious self-management. Vallabha's reading says these are prasada, not achievements. The kshatriya does not manufacture courage; it is given. Your work is to stay open to what flows through you.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's clean inventory is a diagnostic: which of the eight do you actually possess as svabhava and which are you performing? Honest self-reading here — not self-criticism — is itself a bhakti-discipline, the devotee turning clear eyes on the shape of the self Isvara has given.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudan's point that kshatriya-karma is sattvopasarjana-rajoguna-svabhavajam (rajas with sattva as support) is a map for any high-pressure vocation: you need the rajas energy to act and strike, but sattva must be its ground, or rajas collapses into aggression. Build the sattva foundation first, then let the rajas do its work.

self_assessment: All six schools anchored in supplied bhashya prose; Dvaita rendering flagged as inferred (bhashya absent); no smart quotes or unescaped characters; Sridhara content used from clean Sanskrit bhashya only; schema complete.
